2 Analysis sample setup

  • dat_main = fixed main sample from the cleaning script

  • ids_complete = exactly those individuals from this main sample

  • dat_main_final = the same dataset plus order_group

3 Order and carryover

3.1 Inspect session order structure

Check whether the order of the conditions is relevant:

  • whether participants were fasting or satiated first

  • whether order has a main effect

  • whether order moderates the effect of planned_condition

3.6 Order models

Test three models:
A) a baseline model without order;
B) plus the main effect of order;
C) plus the interaction between planned_condition and order_group.

-> If C does not perform better than B, there is no evidence that the condition effect depends on the order.

3.6.1 Baseline vs. additive order model

Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_base: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + (1 | subject)
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_base         4 562.86 570.00 -277.43    554.86                     
m_order_add    5 564.20 573.12 -277.10    554.20 0.6609  1     0.4162

Interpretation

p = .1644 anova(m_base, m_order_add) is not significant –> no statistical evidence that the order has an additional main effect on total_kcal.

3.6.2 Additive order model vs. interaction model

Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_order_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)  
m_order_add    5 564.20 573.12 -277.10    554.20                       
m_order_int    6 563.24 573.94 -275.62    551.24 2.9629  1    0.08519 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

3.6.3 Final order model estimates

Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
   Data: dat_main_order

      AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid

6.1.1 Stress paired test summary

Comparison

< .001

1.48

large

According to the Shapiro–Wilk test, the difference values for “mild” vs. “baseline” and “strong” vs. “baseline” did not differ significantly from a normal distribution (p = .124 and p = .082).

For “strong” vs. “mild” the distribution was not met (p = .0167 -> significant). -> The corresponding Wilcoxon test was also highly significant.

22

t = -4.18

21

< .001

-0.89

large

The metabolic state was more clearly evident in the blood for glucose than for ketones. Although ketone levels were in line with expectations, the evidence is not sufficient in this case. Glucose levels, on the other hand, differed significantly between the conditions.

10 Model specification

10.1 Random effects

First, use a random intercept as the main model

Then test the random slope

  • Only include the random slope if the model is stable

  • no major warnings appear

  • and the fit is visibly better

=> A random-intercept model was retained as the primary specification, as adding a random slope for planned_condition was not considered sufficiently justified given the small sample and two observations per subject.

10.2 Model helpers

Define reporting functions for mixed models

12 Moderation analyses

12.1 Moderation helper functions

12.1.1 Moderation 1: SRS

m_srs_add = main effect of Condition plus main effect of Moderator

m_srs_int = plus interaction; ANOVA tests whether the interaction provides additional explanatory power

fixed

planned_conditionfasted:srs_c

-33.573

69.653

-0.482

22.000

0.635

-178.024

110.878

Intercept -> A person with an average SRS is estimated to consume approximately 372.6 kcal when satiated.

planned_conditionfasted = 2.028 -> With average stress reactivity, it was estimated that approximately 2 kcal more were consumed in the fasted state than in the satiated state. => not significant p > .937

H2a: srs_c -> If srs_c increases by 1 unit, total_kcal is estimated to change by approximately -73.17 kcal in the satiated condition.

H2b: planned_conditionfasted:srs_c -> For every additional unit of SRS, the difference between the fasted and satiated states is estimated to be 48.45 kcal lower. This does not suggest a stronger positive correlation in the fasted state

32.418

H3a was not supported: there was no evidence that adaptive cognitive emotion regulation moderated the association between metabolic condition and total caloric intake. -> direction was even positive

H3b was not supported: maladaptive cognitive emotion regulation was not significantly associated with greater caloric intake, and no significant interaction with metabolic condition was observed. -> direction was even negative

H4a was not supported: trait anxiety was not significantly associated with total caloric intake or high-calorie intake.

H4b was not supported: there was no evidence that trait anxiety moderated the effect of metabolic condition on either total caloric intake or high-calorie intake.

H5 was not supported for restrained eating: EDE-Q restraint was neither significantly associated with total caloric intake nor did it significantly moderate the effect of metabolic condition.
-> but: With increasing restraint in eating, the difference between fasted and satiated states becomes slightly more positive in numerical terms.

H5 was also not supported for stress eating tendencies: SSES showed neither a significant main effect on total caloric intake nor a significant interaction with metabolic condition.

   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    21

Mit allen Sessions:
                          Estimate Std. Error      df    t value
(Intercept)             358.300000   35.56100 27.4623 10.0756434
planned_conditionfasted  -3.450455   23.80128 22.0000 -0.1449693
                                  Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.0000000001005252
planned_conditionfasted 0.8860550466740752

Ohne mismatch Sessions:
                          Estimate Std. Error       df     t value
(Intercept)             355.090952   37.15628 26.22777  9.55668830
planned_conditionfasted  -2.149048   24.89907 21.00000 -0.08631037
                                  Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.0000000004958396
planned_conditionfasted 0.9320373527479284

Excluding sessions with metabolic mismatches did not materially change the main result.

13.2 Log transform

Data: dat
Models:
m0: f_m0
m1: f_m1
   npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m0    3 56.508 61.860 -25.254    50.508                     
m1    4 58.011 65.147 -25.005    50.011 0.4971  1     0.4808
                           Estimate Std. Error      df    t value
(Intercept)              5.77349832  0.1167663 26.9935 49.4448863
planned_conditionfasted -0.05317473  0.0749965 22.0000 -0.7090294
                                                    Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.0000000000000000000000000005095955
planned_conditionfasted 0.4857527798894964998765999553143047
                             2.5 %     97.5 %
.sig01                          NA         NA
.sigma                          NA         NA
(Intercept)              5.5446405 6.00235614
planned_conditionfasted -0.2001652 0.09381572

The null effect remained unchanged when total caloric intake was log-transformed, indicating that the main finding was not driven by skewness of the outcome distribution.

13.3 Outlier check

13.3.1 Sample trimming rule

Extreme observations were defined as sessions above the 99th percentile of total caloric intake.

13.3.2 Primary model including all observations

0.91634551968297018

  • with outlier: planned_conditionfasted = 2.03, p = .938

  • without outlier: planned_conditionfasted = 9.13, p = .738

The null effect of metabolic condition persisted after excluding extreme high-intake observations, suggesting that the main finding was not driven by outliers.
